retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Macrodontia often presents an aesthetic concern in the anterior maxilla, while macrodonts in the posterior region may contribute to crowding and impede normal tooth eruption. Radiographic monitoring is essential to assess potential eruption abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Microdontia occurs more frequently in female individuals, with an overall incidence of 1.5% to 2%. [11] The maxillary lateral incisor is most commonly affected, often referred to as a "peg lateral." [12] Macrodontia has no sex predilection and has been reported in permanent teeth, with a prevalence ranging from 0.03% to 1.9%. [13] This condition typically presents in individuals aged 8 to 13, coinciding with the eruption of permanent dentition.

retrieved_docs.title: Cockayne Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Dental Abnormalities
retrieved_docs.content: The deciduous teeth may appear late and exhibit characteristics such as being small, irregular, and abnormally spaced. In certain instances, permanent teeth may not develop; when they do, they remain small or poorly implanted. Children affected by this condition are more prone to cavities than the average population.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on its severity and extent, microdontia may present aesthetic or functional challenges. In most cases, underlying syndromes cause greater difficulties than the dental condition itself. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental disturbances affecting the shape and size of teeth can significantly alter dental anatomy and necessitate tailored treatment. Some of these conditions primarily present as cosmetic concerns, while others complicate dental care, highlighting the importance of early diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Taurodontism is identified radiographically by enlarged pulp chambers and shortened roots, features that are not clinically observable. Fusion is typically detected during routine dental examinations or when it affects the aesthetic zone, where a single large tooth occupies the space of 2. Patients may remain unaware of the condition unless it presents an aesthetic or functional concern.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for each anomalous condition is outlined below. Additional diagnostic tools, such as radiographic evaluation and genetic testing, may aid in distinguishing between these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperdontia is a condition characterized by additional teeth within the normal dentition, termed supernumerary teeth. Supernumerary teeth are frequently located in the anterior maxilla, between the central incisors, termed mesiodens. [33] In permanent dentition, the prevalence of supernumerary teeth ranges between 0.5% to 5.3%. [34] [35] It affects males more frequently than females, with a ratio of 2 to 1. This phenomenon is often associated with syndromes such as Ehlers-Danlos, cleidocranial dysplasia, and Gardner syndrome. However, it may also be seen in non-syndromic patients. It is also more commonly seen in males than females. As a result of their formation in any part of the dental arch, this can lead to complications, including delayed eruption, malposition, impaction, diastemas, crowding, and poor aesthetics. [33] [34] [35] True anodontia is the absence of primary teeth, and total anodontia is complete tooth agenesis in both the primary and permanent dentition, both of which are extremely rare. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Preadolescents
retrieved_docs.content: Use of tongue blade: This method can correct the development of crossbite. A tongue blade resembles a flat ice cream stick placed inside the mouth, contacting the erupting tooth in a crossbite on its palatal side. During the slight closure of the jaws, the opposing side of the tooth comes in contact with the labial aspect of the opposing mandibular tooth. Light forces generated during this period might help the tooth attain a better position. Catalan’s appliance or lower inclined plane: The lower inclined plane is constructed at an angle of 45 to the maxillary occlusal plane and can be cemented on lower incisors. Face masks and Rapid maxillary expansion: This method can be used when a skeletal-transverse deficiency occurs in the maxilla Frankel III appliance: Can be used to correct a developing Class III skeletal malocclusion. Chin cup appliance: It can be used to redirect the growth of a prognathic mandible.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 997
retrieved_docs.content: Intervillous hemorrhage of placenta is a disorder characterized by bleeding into the placental intervillous space and is regarded as a condition affecting the fetal membranes or amniotic fluid; it occurs in the context of a pregnancy in which a child is present.
